title: Reference vs Value Check
description: Predict when variables share objects vs copy primitives.

questions.explanation: Primitives copy by value. Reassigning `b` does not change `a`.
`b = a` creates an alias. Mutating through `b` changes the same object that `a` references.
`b = { n: 999 }` is reassignment (new object), not mutation. `a.n = 2` mutates the original object only.

questions.prompt: Which assignment creates an **alias** (two variables referencing the same object)?

options.text: `const b = a` when `a` is an object
`let b = a` when `a` is a number
`let b = a + 1` when `a` is a number

questions.explanation: Assigning an object variable to another creates a reference alias. Spread creates a new top-level object (shallow copy), and primitives copy by value.

questions.prompt: Which of these is **mutation** (changing an existing value in place)?

options.text: `let b = a` when `a` is a number

questions.explanation: Setting a property on an existing object mutates it. Reassignment and spread create new bindings or containers.
